findings.title: SSRF via untrusted sessionWebhook URL in DingTalk sendMessage
findings.description: The defaultWebhookSender function issues an HTTP POST to an arbitrary URL. The sessionWebhook URL originates from incoming DingTalk bot messages (line 186-190), which is external untrusted input. An attacker who controls message content could craft a sessionWebhook pointing to internal services (e.g., http://169.254.169.254 for cloud metadata, or internal APIs), causing Server-Side Request Forgery. The getSessionWebhook method (line 162) makes this URL accessible to callers, and sendMessage (line 137) passes the target directly to defaultWebhookSender without validation.
findings.suggestion: Validate the webhook URL before making the request: (1) Parse with new URL(webhookUrl), (2) Allowlist the hostname to oapi.dingtalk.com or a configurable set of allowed domains, (3) Reject private/internal IP ranges. Example: create a validateWebhookUrl(url: string, allowedHosts: string[]) function that throws on invalid URLs.

findings.title: Package has zero consumers -- all new channels are orphan code
findings.code_snippet:
export { DingTalkChannel } from './dingtalk/index.js'
export { EmailChannel } from './email/index.js'
export { FeishuChannel } from './feishu/index.js'
findings.description: No package.json in the monorepo depends on @proj-airi/channels-extra. grep for '@proj-airi/channels-extra' across all .ts files returns zero hits. The three new channel implementations (EmailChannel, FeishuChannel, DingTalkChannel) are exported but not imported or wired to any entry point (HTTP handler, bot service, Electron main process, CLI). This is dead-on-arrival code per the integration rules. The existing SlackChannel and WhatsAppChannel have the same problem, suggesting the package itself has never been integrated.
findings.suggestion: Wire at least one channel to a live entry point to prove end-to-end connectivity. For example, register EmailChannel in an Electron main service or a server-runtime plugin, and add the @proj-airi/channels-extra dependency to that consumer's package.json. Without this, the vertical slice is incomplete.

findings.title: DingTalk handleBotMessage returns SUCCESS on parse error
findings.description: When JSON.parse or handler processing fails, handleBotMessage returns { status: 'SUCCESS' } to the DingTalk stream client. This tells the platform the message was successfully processed when it was not. The platform will not retry delivery, causing silent message loss. This is the error-as-valid-state anti-pattern seen in ScreenshotPipeline.tick() and consumption layer modules.
findings.suggestion: Return a failure status so the platform can retry: return { status: 'LATER', message: `parse error: ${(err as Error).message}` }. Alternatively, if the Stream protocol requires SUCCESS, at minimum emit an error event or invoke an onError callback so callers can detect the failure.

findings.title: Email disconnect: partial cleanup on error leaks resources
findings.description: disconnect() performs three sequential cleanup steps (mailboxLock.release, imapClient.logout, smtpTransport.close) in a single try block. If imapClient.logout() throws, smtpTransport is never closed and its reference is never nulled. This is a resource leak. The same pattern was found in prior iterations with McpHub.shutdown() (now fixed with try/finally).
findings.suggestion: Use a finally block or individual try/catch for each resource. Pattern: try { mailboxLock.release() } catch { log } finally { this.mailboxLock = null }; try { await imapClient.logout() } catch { log } finally { this.imapClient = null }; etc. Set status and re-throw at the end if any failed.

findings.title: Email connect/disconnect/send error paths not tested
findings.description: The email channel test covers the happy path for connect, disconnect, and sendMessage, but does not test error scenarios where the underlying IMAP client or SMTP transport throw errors. In EmailChannel.connect() (line 109), connect errors are caught, status set to 'error', and rethrown. In sendMessage(), SMTP sendMail failure is not tested. These are important error paths -- if IMAP/SMTP credentials are wrong or the server is down, the channel must correctly transition to error state and propagate the error. Without these tests, error handling regressions can reach production undetected.
findings.suggestion: Add tests that configure the mock IMAP client's connect() to reject, and verify: (1) channel.status becomes 'error', (2) the error is propagated with correct message and cause. Similarly test SMTP sendMail rejection after connect.

findings.title: Email sendMessage: no try/catch on SMTP I/O
findings.description: sendMessage() validates preconditions but lets the smtpTransport.sendMail() call propagate raw library errors to callers. The error will have no context about the email target, sender, or channel state. This matches the recurring pattern in this codebase where I/O methods lack try/catch wrapping.
findings.suggestion: Wrap in try/catch and throw a contextual error: catch (error) { throw new Error(`Email send to ${target} failed`, { cause: error }) }

findings.title: DingTalk sessionWebhooks Map grows without bound
findings.code_snippet: private sessionWebhooks = new Map<string, SessionWebhookEntry>()
findings.description: The sessionWebhooks Map accumulates entries for every unique conversationId received. Entries are only removed on individual expiry check (getSessionWebhook) or disconnect. There is no size cap, no periodic eviction, and no LRU policy. In a high-traffic DingTalk bot, this Map will grow indefinitely as new conversations arrive, causing memory pressure. The expiry check in getSessionWebhook (line 166) only fires on read -- expired entries for conversations never queried again remain in memory forever.
findings.suggestion: Add a maximum size cap (e.g., 10000 entries) with LRU eviction, or implement periodic cleanup of expired entries. Consider using a TTL-aware Map implementation. At minimum, add a sweepExpired() method called periodically or on every N insertions.

findings.title: Feishu sendMessage: no try/catch on API call, response code unchecked
findings.description: Two issues: (1) The Feishu API call has no try/catch, so raw SDK errors propagate without channel context. (2) The Feishu API returns { code: number } indicating API-level success/failure, but the response code is never checked. A non-zero code means the message was not delivered, yet sendMessage will appear to succeed.
findings.suggestion: Wrap in try/catch with context. Also check the response: const result = await this.client.im.message.create(...); if (result.code !== 0) { throw new Error(`Feishu API error code ${result.code}, target: ${target}`) }

findings.title: Feishu/DingTalk connect error paths and send failures not tested
findings.description: Both FeishuChannel and DingTalkChannel have catch blocks in connect() that set status to 'error' and rethrow, but no tests exercise these paths. Additionally, FeishuChannel.sendMessage() calls client.im.message.create() which could reject or return a non-zero code -- neither failure mode is tested. DingTalkChannel.sendMessage() delegates to sendViaWebhook which could reject -- not tested.
findings.suggestion: Add error-path tests for both channels. For Feishu, test wsClient.start() throwing and verify error status. For DingTalk, test streamClient.connect() rejecting. Also test sendMessage failures.

findings.title: Channel deps optional in constructor, checked at connect() runtime
findings.description: All three new channel classes (EmailChannel, FeishuChannel, DingTalkChannel) accept deps as optional constructor parameter, storing null if missing. The invariant 'a channel must have its dependencies to function' is deferred to connect() where it throws at runtime. This creates a period where the object exists in an invalid state: it has been constructed but cannot perform any useful work.
findings.suggestion: Make deps a required constructor parameter. If DI requires lazy creation, use a factory function that validates deps before constructing the channel: export function createEmailChannel(config: EmailConfig, deps: EmailChannelDeps): EmailChannel.

findings.title: No email address validation on sendMessage target
findings.description: The sendMessage method accepts an arbitrary string as the target email address and passes it directly to SMTP sendMail without any validation. This could lead to SMTP header injection if the target contains newlines or special characters. Additionally, malformed addresses could cause SMTP errors that are difficult to diagnose.
findings.suggestion: Validate the target email address before passing it to sendMail. Use a validation library (e.g., the 'validator' package isEmail function, or a Valibot email schema) to verify the format. At minimum, reject addresses containing \r or \n characters.

findings.title: Email fetchNewMessages: fire-and-forget with log-only catch
findings.code_snippet:
this.fetchNewMessages(existsData.prevCount + 1, existsData.count)
  .catch(err => logger.withError(err as Error).error('fetch new messages failed'))
findings.description: fetchNewMessages is invoked as fire-and-forget from the IMAP 'exists' event handler. Errors are caught and logged but never surface to the channel's error state. If message fetching consistently fails, the channel remains in 'connected' status while silently dropping all new messages. There is no onError callback, no status change to 'error', and no retry mechanism.
findings.suggestion: At minimum, set status to 'error' on repeated fetch failures, or add an onError callback to the channel class. Consider a retry with exponential backoff before transitioning to error state.

findings.title: Feishu EventDispatcher depends on SDK internal _handlers Map
findings.description: The FeishuEventDispatcher interface models the Feishu SDK's internal implementation detail (_handlers is a private Map). The underscore prefix convention indicates this is not a public API. If the Feishu SDK changes its internal structure, this code will silently break at runtime. The createEventDispatcher method (line 158) constructs this internal structure directly, bypassing any official SDK registration API.
findings.suggestion: Use the official Feishu SDK EventDispatcher constructor and its public registration API instead of constructing the internal _handlers Map directly. If the SDK requires this pattern, add a comment documenting the specific SDK version this depends on.

findings.title: DingTalk sendMessage: no try/catch on webhook sender I/O
findings.description: sendMessage delegates to either a custom webhook sender or defaultWebhookSender. Both can throw network errors, but sendMessage has no try/catch to add channel context (target, platform, status) to the error. Raw fetch errors like 'TypeError: fetch failed' will propagate without context about the DingTalk channel operation.
findings.suggestion: Wrap in try/catch: catch (error) { throw new Error(`DingTalk send to ${target} failed`, { cause: error }) }

findings.title: Handler dispatch loop duplicated identically across all 3 channels
findings.description: The handler dispatch loop (iterating over messageHandlers, invoking each, catching sync errors, and attaching .catch to async results) is copied verbatim across email/index.ts (lines 198-208), feishu/index.ts (lines 197-207), and dingtalk/index.ts (lines 204-214). This is 11 lines x 3 = 33 lines of near-identical code.
findings.suggestion: Extract to a shared dispatchToHandlers(handlers, message, logger) utility in packages/channels-extra/src/channel-utils.ts. Each channel then calls: dispatchToHandlers(this.messageHandlers, incomingMessage, logger)

findings.title: setStatus method duplicated identically across all 3 channels
findings.description: The setStatus method is identical in all three channel classes. Along with setStatus, the fields _status, statusHandlers, onStatusChange, messageHandlers, and onMessage are also identical across all three. Together these account for ~35 lines of duplicated boilerplate per class (~105 lines total).
findings.suggestion: Extract a BaseChannel abstract class or a createHandlerRegistry() composition utility to consolidate the ~105 lines of identical boilerplate.

findings.title: Hardcoded email subject line 'Message from Anima'
findings.code_snippet: subject: 'Message from Anima',
findings.description: The email subject is hardcoded to 'Message from Anima'. This should be configurable -- different deployments or contexts may need different subject lines. The MessageContent type only has a 'text' field, so there is no way for callers to specify a subject.
findings.suggestion: Add an optional 'subject' field to MessageContent, or add a 'defaultSubject' field to EmailConfig, or accept a subject parameter in sendMessage.

findings.title: Missing JSDoc on exported EmailChannelDeps interface
findings.description: EmailChannelDeps is exported and serves as the dependency injection contract for EmailChannel, but has no documentation explaining what implementations should provide or when consumers need to supply it.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc explaining the interface purpose: /** Dependencies for EmailChannel. Callers must supply factory functions that create IMAP and SMTP clients configured with credentials from EmailConfig. */

findings.title: Missing JSDoc on exported FeishuChannelDeps interface
findings.description: Same convention gap as EmailChannelDeps. This exported interface is the DI contract for FeishuChannel but lacks any JSDoc.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc: /** Dependencies for FeishuChannel. Supply factories wrapping @larksuiteoapi/node-sdk Client and WSClient instances. */

findings.title: Missing JSDoc on exported DingTalkChannelDeps interface
findings.description: Same convention gap. DingTalkChannelDeps is the DI contract but has no JSDoc. The optional sendViaWebhook field is especially non-obvious.
findings.suggestion: Add JSDoc: /** Dependencies for DingTalkChannel. Supply a factory for dingtalk-stream's DWClient. Optionally override sendViaWebhook (defaults to fetch-based POST to session webhook URL). */

findings.title: Feishu JSON.parse catch discards error object
findings.description: The catch block for JSON.parse does not bind the error variable. While it logs context (messageId), the actual parse error message is lost, making debugging malformed messages harder than necessary.
findings.suggestion: Bind the error and include it in the log: catch (err) { logger.withFields({ messageId: message.message_id }).withError(err as Error).warn('failed to parse message content') }

findings.title: Unsafe 'as' cast on IMAP 'exists' event data
findings.code_snippet:
this.imapClient.on('exists', (data: unknown) => {
      const existsData = data as { path: string, count: number, prevCount: number }
findings.description: The IMAP 'exists' event handler casts unknown data to a specific shape without runtime validation. If the imapflow library changes the event payload shape, this will silently produce incorrect behavior (e.g., NaN from undefined arithmetic on line 169).
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime type guard that validates the data shape before accessing its properties. Return early with a warning log if the shape doesn't match.

findings.title: Unsafe `as` cast on IMAP exists event data without type guard
findings.code_snippet: const existsData = data as { path: string, count: number, prevCount: number }
findings.description: The IMAP client 'exists' event handler receives `data: unknown` and immediately casts it with `as` to an assumed shape. If the IMAP library changes its event payload shape or sends unexpected data, this will silently produce undefined values used in arithmetic, potentially causing NaN propagation or incorrect message fetching.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime type guard: function isExistsData(d: unknown): d is { path: string, count: number, prevCount: number } { ... }. Use it before accessing properties, log and return early if guard fails.

findings.title: connect() error-handling pattern duplicated across all 3 channels
findings.description: All three connect() methods follow the identical pattern: setStatus('connecting') -> guard on !this.deps -> try { channel-specific setup; setStatus('connected') } catch { setStatus('error'); throw }. The disconnect() methods follow a similar pattern.
findings.suggestion: Extract a connectWithGuard(setup, depsDescription) method in BaseChannel that handles the status transitions and error wrapping, letting each subclass supply only the channel-specific setup logic.

findings.title: DingTalk expired session webhook not tested
findings.code_snippet:
it('should store session webhook from incoming message for later use', async () => {
  // ... only tests non-expired webhook retrieval
findings.description: DingTalkChannel.getSessionWebhook() has an expiry check (line 166): if Date.now() > entry.expiresAt, it deletes the entry and returns undefined. This boundary condition is not tested.
findings.suggestion: Add a test with sessionWebhookExpiredTime set to a past timestamp and verify getSessionWebhook returns undefined.

findings.title: Feishu malformed message content and empty text not tested
findings.code_snippet:
it('should receive messages and invoke handler', async () => {
  // Only tests well-formed JSON content
findings.description: FeishuChannel.handleIncomingMessage() has two untested boundary conditions: (1) JSON.parse failure on message.content, (2) empty text after parsing. Neither path is exercised.
findings.suggestion: Add tests for malformed JSON content and empty text content, verifying no handler is called in each case.

findings.title: Three channels added with no vertical slice integration
findings.code_snippet:
export { DingTalkChannel } from './dingtalk/index.js'
export { EmailChannel } from './email/index.js'
export { FeishuChannel } from './feishu/index.js'
findings.description: This change adds three horizontal channel implementations simultaneously without delivering any end-to-end vertical slice. None of the three channels is wired to a use case, registered in a service, or connected to any UI configuration. This is the 'horizontal layer' anti-pattern.
findings.suggestion: Pick one channel (e.g., EmailChannel) and wire it end-to-end: register it in a service, add config UI, and prove a message can flow through. Then add the remaining channels incrementally.

findings.title: Unsafe `as DingTalkBotMessage` cast on JSON.parse result
findings.code_snippet: const message = JSON.parse(event.data) as DingTalkBotMessage
findings.description: JSON.parse returns `any` which is cast to DingTalkBotMessage without validation. A structurally wrong payload would lead to undefined values propagating into IncomingMessage and session webhook storage.
findings.suggestion: Add a minimal runtime shape check after JSON.parse: verify typeof message.msgId === 'string' && typeof message.conversationId === 'string' && typeof message.text?.content === 'string' before proceeding.

findings.title: Email extractTextFromSource edge case not tested
findings.code_snippet:
it('should receive messages via IMAP and invoke handler', async () => {
  // Only tests the happy path with properly formatted email source
findings.description: EmailChannel.extractTextFromSource() handles the case where no '\r\n\r\n' separator is found -- it returns the raw content as-is. This boundary condition is not tested.
findings.suggestion: Add a test where _simulateNewEmail provides a source Buffer without the '\r\n\r\n' separator and verify the raw content is returned.

findings.title: Naive email body extraction ignores MIME encoding
findings.description: The extractTextFromSource method splits on the first \r\n\r\n boundary and returns everything after as text. This ignores MIME multipart messages, Content-Transfer-Encoding, and charset encoding. Real-world emails are almost always MIME-encoded, so this will produce garbled text for most incoming messages.
findings.suggestion: Use the 'mailparser' library (simpleParser or parseMessage) to properly extract text content from email source. This is a classic NIH violation.

findings.title: IncomingMessage remains mutable despite fan-out to multiple handlers
findings.code_snippet:
export interface IncomingMessage {
  id: string
  platform: ChannelPlatform
  channelId: string
  senderId: string
  senderName?: string
  text: string
  timestamp: Date
  raw?: unknown
}
findings.description: IncomingMessage is constructed in each channel and passed to multiple handlers sequentially. Any handler could mutate the message object before the next handler sees it, leading to subtle cross-handler interference. Previously flagged in Task 16 review and remains unfixed.
findings.suggestion: Add readonly to all fields or Object.freeze() before dispatching.

findings.title: DingTalk handleBotMessage JSON parse error not tested
findings.code_snippet:
it('should receive messages and invoke handler', async () => {
  // Only tests well-formed JSON event data
findings.description: DingTalkChannel.handleBotMessage() wraps the entire body in try/catch. If event.data contains invalid JSON, the catch logs the error and returns { status: 'SUCCESS', message: 'parse error' }. This error path is not tested.
findings.suggestion: Test the parse-error path by calling the handler with invalid JSON data and verify the return value is { status: 'SUCCESS', message: 'parse error' }.

findings.title: Email from[0] access on potentially empty array
findings.code_snippet:
const from = msg.envelope.from[0]

const incomingMessage: IncomingMessage = {
  ...
  channelId: from?.address ?? 'unknown',
  senderId: from?.address ?? 'unknown',
findings.description: msg.envelope.from[0] can be undefined if the from array is empty. The optional chaining silently converts this to 'unknown', making the message appear to come from an unknown sender rather than flagging the anomaly.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit check: if (!msg.envelope.from?.length) { logger.warn('email with no from address'); continue; }

findings.title: Config interfaces accept any values without validation at boundary
findings.description: EmailConfig, FeishuConfig, and DingTalkConfig are accepted by channel constructors without any runtime validation. Ports could be negative, addresses could be empty strings. These are Imperative Shell boundary types receiving external configuration.
findings.suggestion: Define Valibot schemas for each config type and validate in a factory function or at the ChannelRegistry.register() boundary.

findings.title: onStatusChange unsubscribe not tested in any channel
findings.code_snippet:
channel.onStatusChange(s => statuses.push(s))

await channel.connect()

expect(statuses).toContain('connecting')
findings.description: All three channels implement onStatusChange() returning an unsubscribe function. While onMessage unsubscribe is tested, onStatusChange unsubscribe is never tested. A bug in unsubscribe logic could cause stale handlers to continue receiving status updates.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that subscribes, unsubscribes, then performs connect/disconnect and verifies the handler is no longer invoked.

findings.title: Feishu JSON.parse cast on message content without structural check
findings.code_snippet: const parsed = JSON.parse(message.content) as { text?: string }
findings.description: The Feishu message content is JSON-parsed and cast to { text?: string }. The cast could mask non-text message types that parse successfully but have a different shape. The message_type field is available but is not checked before parsing as text.
findings.suggestion: Check message.message_type === 'text' before attempting to parse as text content.

findings.title: Error messages include user-supplied target in plaintext
findings.description: Error messages in sendMessage across all three channels include the target parameter verbatim. For Email this is an email address, for DingTalk this is a webhook URL. If these errors propagate to logs or user-facing responses, they could expose PII or internal URLs.
findings.suggestion: Truncate or mask the target in error messages. For email: mask the address. For webhook URLs: log only the domain.

findings.title: All channels: message handlers log-only with no failure surfacing
findings.description: All three channels use the same pattern: iterate handlers, catch errors, log them. This is correct for fault isolation, but there is no way for the channel owner to observe handler failures.
findings.suggestion: Add an optional onHandlerError callback to the channel constructor or Channel interface.

findings.title: StatusChangeHandler type alias duplicated across all 3 channel files
findings.code_snippet: type StatusChangeHandler = (status: ChannelStatus) => void
findings.description: The type alias `StatusChangeHandler = (status: ChannelStatus) => void` is independently defined in all three channel files. This duplication is a minor DRY concern but could diverge over time.
findings.suggestion: Export StatusChangeHandler from types.ts alongside the other shared types.

findings.title: StatusChangeHandler type duplicated across all 3 files
findings.code_snippet: type StatusChangeHandler = (status: ChannelStatus) => void
findings.description: The type alias is independently declared in each of the three channel files. This is a shared contract that belongs in the shared types module.
findings.suggestion: Move to packages/channels-extra/src/types.ts and import in each channel file.

findings.title: Public Channel methods lack JSDoc across all three channels
findings.description: Only DingTalkChannel.sendMessage and DingTalkChannel.getSessionWebhook have JSDoc. The other public methods across all three channel classes are undocumented.
findings.suggestion: Add minimal JSDoc to at least connect() and onMessage() on the Channel interface in types.ts.

findings.title: Inline comment restates obvious code in test helper
findings.code_snippet: // Extract the registered handler from the event dispatcher
findings.description: This comment restates what the immediately following line of code does. The code is straightforward enough that the comment adds maintenance cost without comprehension benefit.
findings.suggestion: Remove the comment. The code is self-documenting.

positive_observations: Consistent DI pattern across all three channels: constructor deps injection matches existing SlackChannel/WhatsAppChannel convention exactly
All three channels use @guiiai/logg with proper namespaced logger instances (channels-extra:email, channels-extra:feishu, channels-extra:dingtalk)
Test files include proper Test Double rationale comments explaining why external SDK mocking is necessary
Error handling in connect/disconnect follows the established pattern: set error status, throw with cause chain
Message handler dispatch has both sync try/catch and async .catch() to prevent unhandled rejections
DingTalk channel properly handles session webhook expiry with TTL-based validation in getSessionWebhook
Feishu channel correctly filters out bot self-messages to prevent echo loops
Clean separation of interface types (ImapClient, SmtpTransport, FeishuClient, StreamClient) from implementation -- enables proper dependency injection
Error wrapping with { cause } in connect()/disconnect() preserves the error chain -- good pattern applied consistently
Status change handlers wrapped in individual try/catch blocks -- one failing handler does not block others
Guard clauses (null checks + status checks) at the top of sendMessage methods provide clear precondition validation with contextual error messages
DingTalk defaultWebhookSender checks response.ok and throws with HTTP status context -- proper fetch error handling
ChannelConfig discriminated union properly extended with EmailConfig | FeishuConfig | DingTalkConfig -- each variant has a literal platform discriminant field
ChannelPlatform literal union correctly extended in lockstep with config variants -- 5-value union matches 5 config interfaces
FeishuConfig.receiveIdType is a proper 3-value literal union ('chat_id' | 'open_id' | 'user_id') instead of plain string
ChannelRegistry.getByPlatform() now returns spread copy with Readonly wrapper -- FIXED from Task 16's mutable reference leak (P1)
ChannelRegistry.register() now validates channel.platform matches config.platform -- FIXED from Task 16's missing validation (P1)
All three channel classes are well-structured with consistent API surface matching the Channel interface
Guard clauses used effectively (early return in setupExistsListener, handleIncomingMessage bot-filter)
Function lengths are reasonable: no function exceeds 35 lines
Maximum nesting depth is 3, within acceptable thresholds
Structured logging used consistently with contextual fields (no console.log)
Good coverage of core happy paths: connect/disconnect lifecycle, send/receive messages, handler subscribe/unsubscribe for all three channels
DingTalk test covers non-text message filtering and text trimming edge cases
Feishu test covers bot message filtering (sender_type === 'bot') and custom receiveIdType configuration
No forbidden patterns (TODO, FIXME, HACK, PLACEHOLDER, TEMP) found in any of the six files
Class-level JSDoc on all three channel classes is concise and accurately describes the communication protocol used
